indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: EM-DAT (International Disaster Database), CRED, UCLouvain
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Score(c,t) = minmax_t(0.5 × z_deaths + 0.5 × z_affected). Z-score standardization handles extreme right-skew. Equal weighting captures both intensity (deaths) and breadth (total affected). Min-max within year produces 0-1 score. Countries with no recorded disasters coded 0.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Z-score standardization of sub-components, then within-year min-max rescaling to 0-1
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Reporting bias: disasters in wealthier countries are better documented. Deaths and affected populations may be underestimated in conflict zones.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Acute natural disasters destroy livelihoods, displace populations, and create desperation that makes coercive labor offers viable. Post-disaster environments generate demand for low-skilled labor in unregulated reconstruction. Displaced populations lose financial and social support networks, reducing resilience.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: ACLED (Armed Conflict Location & Event Data Project)
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Score(c,t) = minmax_t(0.4 × norm(ln(intensity)) + 0.3 × civilian_ratio + 0.3 × norm(ln(spread))). Three sub-dimensions capture intensity (event/fatality counts), targeting (civilian share), and geographic breadth (number of admin1 units affected). Log-transformation tames heavy right tail.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Log-transform of sub-components, then within-year min-max rescaling to 0-1
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: ACLED coverage varies by region and year. Civilian targeting ratio depends on event coding methodology.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Armed conflict induces state collapse, deteriorates rule of law, and creates humanitarian needs. Conflict-related trafficking is used by armed groups to control communities and support hostilities. State collapse and impunity allow traffickers to operate without institutional constraint.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: ND-GAIN (Notre Dame Global Adaptation Initiative)
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Score(c,t) = NDGAIN_VULN_01(c,t). ND-GAIN vulnerability sub-index, min-max rescaled within year. Trend variable retained for Stage 3 interaction modeling with disaster impact.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Within-year min-max rescaling of ND-GAIN vulnerability sub-index to 0-1
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Functions as chronic amplifier of acute shocks rather than independent additive component. Stage 3 may model this as interaction term with 1.1.1.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Climate stress serves as a chronic amplifier of acute shocks. Slow-onset events undermine rights to food, water, and health, driving migration or displacement. Climate-induced mobility often leads to irregular migration pathways where individuals face heightened abuse and exploitation risk.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: IDMC (Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre) / UNHCR
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Geocoded IDP coordinate locations with population counts, normalized per capita. Min-max rescaling to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Full GIS implementation deferred. National-level proxy used in current aggregation. Subnational geocoded data preserved for Layer 3 spatial analysis.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Internally displaced populations are spatially concentrated in locations with elevated recruiter access and reduced institutional protection. Displacement destroys social networks and economic stability, creating ideal conditions for coercive recruitment.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: IOM Missing Migrants Project
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Deaths per estimated migrants on route. Geocoded corridor-level data, aggregated to national level. Min-max rescaled to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Route danger is a corridor-level measure. National aggregation loses geographic specificity. Full GIS implementation deferred.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Migrant route danger provides a structural signature of unregulated migration. Dangerous corridors indicate pathways where institutional protection is absent and traffickers can operate with impunity along transit routes.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: ILO (International Labour Organization) — ILOSTAT
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Score(c,t) = minmax_t(YOUTH_EXCL_AVG ?? YOUTH_EXCL_BEST). Composite of youth unemployment rate and NEET rate (not in education, employment, or training). Average where both exist; best-available fallback extends coverage to 1991.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Within-year min-max rescaling of composite to 0-1
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Sub-index construction (PCA vs. simple average) pending Stage 2 determination. ILO modeled estimates used where survey data unavailable.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Youth with no structured institutional anchor (employment or education) are the population most accessible to traffickers. Developmental vulnerabilities make youth easily exploitable. Youth experiencing homelessness are significantly overrepresented in trafficking statistics.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: ILO / UNICEF — Global Estimates of Child Labour
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Score(c,t) = minmax_global(CL_RATE_BEST). Proportion of children in employment. Global min-max normalization.
indicatorMetadata.Temporal Coverage: Most recent survey year per country
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: CRITICAL: Only ~100 countries have data. Missing data must carry through as missing — not imputed to zero. Coverage gaps concentrated in high-income countries.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Child labor rates signal both direct vulnerability of children to exploitation and deep structural household desperation that drives families to accept exploitative arrangements. Functions as a precursor to future human capital loss and continued exploitation cycles.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: World Bank — World Development Indicators (WDI), Gini Index
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Score(c,t) = GINI_01. Natural 0-1 scale (Gini coefficient divided by 100). Observed range 0.20-0.71. No further rescaling required.
indicatorMetadata.Temporal Coverage: Varies by country; most recent available year used
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Functions as amplifier on poverty — concentrates vulnerability in lower distribution. Tagged for Stage 3 interaction modeling with poverty incidence (1.3.4).
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Inequality amplifies poverty by concentrating vulnerability in the lower end of the income distribution. High inequality means a larger share of the population faces constrained economic choices, even if average income levels are moderate.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: World Bank — World Development Indicators (WDI)
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Score(c,t) = EDU_DEFICIT_01. Deficit inversion: (100 − lower secondary completion rate) / 100, capped at 1. Score of 0 = full completion; score of 1 = no completion.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Deficit inversion of completion rate to 0-1
indicatorMetadata.Temporal Coverage: Annual (WDI, varies by country survey year)
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Note: In index file this column captures educational deficit, not poverty incidence. The 1.3.4 code in the framework doc refers to poverty incidence, but the actual column name is 'Educational Attainment Gaps'.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Populations with truncated education face a structural ceiling on economic alternatives. Lower secondary completion captures the human capital dimension of constrained choice — without education, individuals have fewer options to refuse exploitative offers.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: World Bank — World Development Indicators (WDI)
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Score(c,t) = REMIT_GDP_NORM. Remittances as % of GDP, min-max rescaled within year. Linear entry; piecewise U-shape transformation deferred to Stage 2/3.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Linear transformation used; piecewise U-shape (capturing both high dependence and structural migration pressure) deferred to Stage 2/3 empirical testing.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Remittance dependence ties household economic survival to out-migration. Cultural norms can compel individuals to endure abuse to avoid shame of returning empty-handed, creating cycles of indebtedness that perpetuate vulnerability to trafficking.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: World Governance Indicators (WGI) — Rule of Law series
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Score(c,t) = RL_DEFICIT_01. Inverted: WGI -2.5 (weakest rule of law) → 1.0; WGI +2.5 (strongest) → 0.0. Formula: (2.5 − RL.EST) / 5.0.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Linear rescaling using theoretical bounds (-2.5 to +2.5)
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Cross-phase usage: same WGI Rule of Law series used in Phase 2 (2.1.1) for enforcement deterrence. Phase 1 captures legal recourse availability; Phase 2 captures enforcement deterrence. Sensitivity analysis flagged for Stage 3.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Captures whether an individual confronted with a coercive recruitment offer has a functional institutional channel to seek remedy or report abuse. Weak rule of law means victims cannot access legal protection before exploitation begins.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: World Bank ID4D (Identification for Development) / Global Findex Database
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Score(c,t) = DOC_DEFICIT_01. Inverted coverage composite: average of (1 - national ID coverage), (1 - birth registration rate), and (1 - formal financial account ownership). Average-of-available method.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Deficit inversion of coverage composite to 0-1
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Dual-use: Phase 1 uses inverted score (deficit); Phase 3 (3.4.1) uses threshold logic above 30%. Sub-index construction (PCA vs. simple average) pending.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Without identity documents, individuals are invisible to protective institutions and vulnerable to deportation threats and exploitation. Documentation is a prerequisite for accessing legal recourse, social services, and formal employment.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: ILO World Social Protection Database / World Bank ASPIRE
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Score(c,t) = SP_GAP_01 ?? minmax_t(SP_DEFICIT_BEST). Social protection coverage gap (1 - coverage rate). Fallback to best-available measure with min-max rescaling.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Deficit inversion; within-year min-max rescaling as fallback
indicatorMetadata.Temporal Coverage: Varies (ILO: latest available; WB ASPIRE: survey-based)
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Coverage definitions vary across sources (ILO vs. WB). Effective coverage vs. legal coverage distinction important.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Social protection operationalizes whether institutional buffers exist that allow individuals to refuse dangerous labor offers. Without unemployment insurance, health coverage, or cash transfer programs, individuals must accept whatever work is available — including exploitative arrangements.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: DEMIG POLICY / Immigration Policies in Comparison (IMPIC)
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Emigration-side policy restrictiveness index. Min-max rescaled to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Limited country coverage. Restrictive emigration policies drive migrants to irregular channels controlled by traffickers.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Restrictive emigration policies foreclose legal movement options and force migrants into unregulated pathways. When legal channels are unavailable, individuals must rely on smugglers and informal networks — the same infrastructure traffickers exploit.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: Walk Free — Global Slavery Index (GSI), Government Response milestones
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Binary or ordinal scoring from GSI government response milestones. Rescaled to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Rescaled to 0-1 from GSI ordinal scale
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: GSI government response data. Amanda addition from Walk Free dataset.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Captures the overall freedom and governance environment affecting protection against coercive recruitment. Weaker freedom protections indicate reduced institutional capacity to prevent trafficking at the recruitment stage.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: World Bank Enterprise Surveys
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: BRIBE_PIT = last IC.FRM.BRIB.ZS / 100. Percentage of firms expected to give gifts to public officials. Divided by 100 to produce 0-1 scale.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Sparse temporal coverage (16.8% of country-years). Enterprise Survey waves are irregular. Uses most recent available observation.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Bribery prevalence directly measures whether traffickers can purchase non-enforcement from officials. Where bribery is routine, exploitation operations can buy protection from inspection, prosecution, and regulatory enforcement.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: ILO (labour inspection ratios) + ITUC Global Rights Index
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: LABOR_IMP_AVG = 0.5 × ILO_INV_01 + 0.5 × ITUC_01. ILO labour inspection data inverted (fewer inspectors = higher risk) and ITUC rights violations index (higher = more violations). Equal-weighted composite.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Component-level normalization to 0-1, then equal-weighted average
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: 42 countries with missing data + weak governance (WGI RoL below threshold) receive elevated imputation floor. Sub-index PCA vs. simple average pending.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Labor regulatory impunity measures whether exploitative labor practices carry any realistic detection or sanction risk. Weak labor inspection capacity and systematic workers' rights violations create environments where forced labor can persist undetected.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: US Department of State — Trafficking in Persons Report
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Tier ranking rescaled: Tier 1 = 0 (best), Tier 3 = 1 (worst). Tier 2 and Tier 2 Watch List receive intermediate scores.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: US TIP rankings reflect US foreign policy assessments. Potential political bias in tier assignments. Useful as complement to objective indicators.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): The TIP Report tier ranking provides an expert assessment of government anti-trafficking effort. Lower tiers indicate governments that are not meeting minimum standards for elimination of trafficking — a direct measure of enforcement capacity deficit.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: GIS-derived friction surface / UNCTAD LSCI (Liner Shipping Connectivity Index)
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: UNCTAD LSCI percentile rank / 100 (annual average Q1-Q4). Higher connectivity used as proxy for concealment capacity in the current national-level implementation.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Full GIS friction surface implementation deferred. Current measure uses maritime connectivity as national-level proxy. Risk direction requires explicit citation on container-based trafficking vectors.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Geographic remoteness and concealment geography reduce the probability of discovery and intervention. Remote areas, maritime zones, and areas with limited infrastructure access provide structural cover for exploitation operations.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: ILO Labour Force Survey (ILOSTAT) — informal employment by sector
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: PC1 of 4-sector informal employment shares (Total, Agriculture, Industry, Services). Standardized, then 0-100 normalized. PC1 explains 93.35% of variance. Alternative: simple average of 4 sectors (0.1% efficiency loss).
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Informal economy percentage from Medina & Schneider (2018) also used — data ends 2015. Temporal currency flag on that component.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): The informal risk exposure score captures the structural depth of informal employment across economic sectors. High informality means exploitation can be embedded in ordinary economic activity without triggering regulatory detection — the core concealment mechanism for forced labor.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: World Bank Enterprise Surveys
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Enterprise Survey measures of workforce precarity including temporary/contract worker prevalence. Normalized to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Sparse temporal coverage due to irregular Enterprise Survey waves.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Workforce precarity measures the structural prevalence of insecure employment arrangements. High precarity means a larger share of workers operate without contracts, protections, or recourse — conditions that facilitate transition from precarious work to forced labor.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: ILO ILOSTAT — sectoral employment shares
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Concentration of employment in sectors known to have high forced labor prevalence (agriculture, construction, domestic work, manufacturing, mining). Weighted by ILO forced labor sector prevalence estimates.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Sector weights based on global forced labor prevalence estimates; may not reflect country-specific patterns.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Economies with high employment concentration in sectors known for forced labor (agriculture, construction, domestic work, mining, fishing) have structural demand for exploitable labor. These sectors are characterized by physical isolation, seasonal labor demand, and weak regulatory oversight.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: UNCTAD — Merchandise Concentration Index (Herfindahl-Hirschman)
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: UNCTAD HHI-based merchandise concentration index. Higher concentration = greater dependence on primary commodity exports. Rescaled to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Relocated from dissolved Phase 4. Captures structural demand for exploitable labor in extractive/agricultural sectors.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Export commodity concentration creates persistent structural demand for cheap, exploitable labor in extractive, agricultural, and manual-intensive sectors. Commodity-dependent economies resist diversification, perpetuating demand that trafficking enterprises supply.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: ILO / World Bank — GDP per worker or output per worker
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Inverted labor productivity: 1 - minmax(productivity). Low productivity economies rely on labor-intensive production where forced labor substitutes for capital investment.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Structural measure of economic model reliance on labor vs. capital.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Low labor productivity indicates economies that rely on labor-intensive production methods rather than capital investment. In these contexts, forced labor provides a cost advantage because exploitation substitutes for technological upgrading.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: Global Initiative Against Transnational Organized Crime (GITOC) — Global Organized Crime Index
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Criminality score minus resilience score from GITOC index. Higher gap = criminality exceeds institutional resilience. Rescaled to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Biennial only. GITOC data also used for 2.4.4 (CA1 sub-dimension). VIF check pending.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): The criminality-resilience gap measures whether organized criminal activity exceeds institutional capacity to contain it. Where criminality outpaces resilience, coercive labor operations can be sustained without effective state intervention.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: World Bank Enterprise Surveys + WGI
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: (PrivateSectorActors_score - 1) / 7.5 × 100, rescaled from GITOC 1-8.5 scale to 0-100, then /100 for 0-1. Captures displacement of public security by private enforcement.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Linear rescaling from GITOC scale to 0-1
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Biennial temporal granularity only. VIF check against GITOC composite pending.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): High private security spending relative to public enforcement indicates displacement of the state's guardianship function. Where private enforcement substitutes for public policing, coercive labor control can operate outside institutional oversight.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: Walk Free — Global Slavery Index (GSI), milestones M4223, M4224NEG
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: GSI government response milestones on document confiscation protections (M4223: laws prohibiting confiscation; M4224NEG: weak enforcement). Composite rescaled to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Original file had ACLED data (concept mismatch). Amanda confirmed GSI data as replacement.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Document confiscation is a primary mechanism of coercive control — workers whose documents are confiscated cannot leave, report abuse, or access services. The legal and enforcement framework around document confiscation determines whether this control mechanism is structurally enabled.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: GITOC — Criminal Actors dimension, CA1 (Mafia-style groups)
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: GITOC CA1 sub-dimension score. Rescaled to 0-1. Captures extent of non-state actor territorial and population control.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Linear rescaling from GITOC scale to 0-1
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Relocated from dissolved Phase 4. GITOC data used elsewhere (2.4.1). CA1 is a distinct sub-dimension — VIF check required.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Non-state actor territorial control displaces state authority. Where armed groups, militias, or organized criminal networks control territory, coercive labor extraction operates without institutional constraint — the state's guardianship function is replaced by an alternative authority that may actively facilitate exploitation.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: Global Findex Database + IMF Financial Access Survey (Household Leverage)
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Recommended: (0.60 × Findex_informal_composite) + (0.40 × IMF_household_leverage). Findex composite: simple average of informal borrowing indicators (fin22b + fin22c) or PC1 (90.1% variance). Both components normalized to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Weighted composite of Findex and IMF components, each normalized to 0-1
indicatorMetadata.Temporal Coverage: Findex: triennial (2011-2021); IMF: annual (86 countries confirmed)
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Proxy measure — no direct cross-national debt bondage data exists. Captures structural conditions enabling debt-based entrapment.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Debt bondage is the most prevalent form of forced labor entrapment globally. High informal borrowing and household leverage create structural conditions where workers become trapped through debt obligations they cannot repay under exploitative terms.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: Walk Free GSI / Henley Passport Index (inverted)
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Passport power inverted — weaker passports = higher entrapment risk. Workers with weak travel documents cannot exit exploitation situations. Rescaled to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Measures structural exit barriers rather than direct confiscation. Complements 2.4.3 (document confiscation risk).
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Passport restrictions create physical barriers to exiting exploitation. Workers with weak travel documents face structural entrapment — they cannot leave the country even if they escape their immediate exploitation situation, making forced labor functionally inescapable.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: Basel Institute on Governance — Basel AML Index
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Basel AML Index / 10 (rescale from original 0-10 to 0-1). Composite of money laundering risk, AML/CFT framework quality, corruption, financial transparency, political/legal risk.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Coverage has expanded over time. Earlier years have fewer countries.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): AML compliance risk measures whether the institutional machinery to detect and disrupt illicit financial flows exists and functions. High AML risk means trafficking proceeds can be integrated into financial systems without triggering detection or asset recovery.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: IMF Financial Access Survey / World Bank Global Financial Development
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: 0.40 × mobile_money + 0.35 × digital_payment + 0.25 × prepaid_share. Weighted composite of non-bank financial channel depth. Each component normalized to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Non-bank financial services provide alternative laundering channels outside traditional banking oversight.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Non-bank financial services depth measures the structural availability of laundering channels that operate outside traditional banking oversight. Mobile money, digital payments, and prepaid instruments provide alternative paths for proceeds concealment.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: World Bank — Global Findex Database
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Mean of Findex cash-only indicators (wage received in cash only, utility payments in cash only, transfer payments in cash only). Each indicator measures share of transactions conducted exclusively in cash.
indicatorMetadata.Normalization Method: Simple mean of cash-only indicator shares
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Triennial data only. Measures structural dominance of cash in transaction environments.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Cash transaction dominance means financial flows carry no automatic intelligence footprint. In cash-dominant economies, trafficking proceeds are structurally invisible to financial monitoring systems, enabling seamless integration into legitimate commerce.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: IMF Financial Access Survey (FAS)
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: PC1 of bank branches + ATMs per 100,000 adults (97.58% variance explained). Coverage: 185-187 countries. NOTE: Risk direction is conditional — dense banking infrastructure enables proceeds management above ~30% account ownership threshold.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Risk direction conditional: banking density increases monetization risk only where account ownership exceeds ~30% threshold. Below threshold, limited banking = limited laundering capacity. Stage 3 threshold testing required.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Banking infrastructure density measures the physical and institutional reach of formal financial channels. Dense banking enables sophisticated proceeds management, layering, and integration — the critical infrastructure for converting exploitation profits into legitimate assets.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: World Bank — Global Findex Database (remittance series)
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Formal vs. informal remittance channel ratio from Findex remittance series. Higher informality = higher risk. Rescaled to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Partial data availability. Scoring formula not fully finalized.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Informal remittance channels provide unmonitored pathways for cross-border value transfer. Where remittances flow primarily through informal networks (hawala, hundi, hand-carry), trafficking proceeds can be moved internationally without triggering financial intelligence reporting.

indicatorMetadata.Primary Data Source: World Bank — Global Findex Database / Enterprise Surveys
indicatorMetadata.Scoring Formula: Formal credit access structure from Findex credit series and Enterprise Survey credit constraint indicators. Rescaled to 0-1.
indicatorMetadata.Key Caveats / Flags: Partial data availability. Scoring formula not fully finalized.
indicatorMetadata.Theoretical Rationale (for popup): Formal credit constraints force individuals and businesses into informal lending markets where trafficking-linked financiers operate. Credit exclusion creates structural dependence on exploitative financial arrangements, including debt bondage schemes.

domainArchitecture.Domain Description: Acute shocks that destroy livelihoods and displace populations, activating latent vulnerability into active trafficking exposure.
domainArchitecture.Indicators Included: 1.1.1 Natural Disaster Impact, 1.1.2 Armed Conflict Exposure, 1.1.3 Climate Stress

domainArchitecture.Domain Description: Spatial structure of forced movement and the geographic locations where that movement concentrates vulnerable populations.
Chronic socioeconomic conditions that eliminate realistic alternatives to exploitative labor offers.
domainArchitecture.Indicators Included: 1.3.1 Youth Labor Market Exclusion, 1.3.2 Child Labor Prevalence, 1.3.3 Economic Inequality, 1.3.4 Educational Attainment Gaps, 1.3.5 Remittance Dependence

domainArchitecture.Domain Description: Absence or inaccessibility of institutional mechanisms that would otherwise protect individuals from coercive recruitment.
domainArchitecture.Indicators Included: 1.5.1 Rule of Law, 1.5.2 Civil Documentation Access, 1.5.3 Social Protection Coverage, 1.5.4 Policy-Constrained Mobility, 1.5.5 Freedom Status

domainArchitecture.Domain Description: Institutional and legal environment determining whether exploitative labor carries any detection or sanction risk, including active state capture.
domainArchitecture.Indicators Included: 2.1.2 Bribery Prevalence, 2.1.3 Labor Regulatory Strength, 2.1.4 US TIP Report Tier Ranking

domainArchitecture.Domain Description: Geographic and spatial conditions that structurally reduce the probability of discovery and intervention.
Economic structures that determine how readily trafficked labor can be absorbed into apparently legitimate activity, including structural demand persistence through commodity dependence.
domainArchitecture.Indicators Included: 2.3.1 Informal Risk Exposure (IRE), 2.3.2 Workforce Precarity, 2.3.3 Concentration of High Risk Sectors, 2.3.4 Export Commodity Concentration, 2.3.5 Labor Productivity (inverted)

domainArchitecture.Domain Description: Institutional conditions under which coercive worker control is operationally feasible, including displacement by non-state authority structures.
domainArchitecture.Indicators Included: 2.4.1 Criminality-Resilience Gap, 2.4.2 PESPI, 2.4.3 Document Confiscation Risk, 2.4.4 Criminal Authority Displacement

domainArchitecture.Domain Description: Economic and physical conditions making exit from exploitation practically impossible.
domainArchitecture.Indicators Included: 2.5.1 Debt Bondage Proxy, 2.5.2 Passport Restriction Index

domainArchitecture.Domain Description: Jurisdictional regulatory and legal framework that enables proceeds concealment through secrecy infrastructure and AML non-compliance.
Structural depth and revealed utilization of laundering channels available to trafficking enterprises.
Structural dominance of transaction environments that carry no automatic financial intelligence footprint.
Physical and institutional reach of formal financial channels enabling proceeds management and victim financial control.
domainArchitecture.Indicators Included: 3.4.1 Banking Infrastructure Density, 3.4.2 Remittance Formality, 3.4.3 Formal Credit Constraint

phaseDefinitions.Definition: Where traffickers gain access to victims. Structural conditions that generate exposed, immobile, or coercible populations.
phaseDefinitions.Theoretical Grounding: Recruitment risk is conceptualized as the interaction of structural stress, forced mobility, constrained economic choice, and recruiter access. The phase draws on crime script analysis (Cornish, 1994), routine activities theory (Cohen & Felson, 1979), and the OSCE business model of trafficking (Aronowitz et al., 2010). Phase 1 captures the convergence of suitable targets (structurally vulnerable populations) in the absence of capable guardians (institutional protection deficits) within environments accessible to motivated offenders.
phaseDefinitions.Key References: Cornish (1994); Cohen & Felson (1979); Aronowitz et al. (2010); Walk Free (2019); UNODC (2021)

phaseDefinitions.Definition: Where labor is extracted under coercion. Conditions that suppress resistance and enable sustained control.
phaseDefinitions.Theoretical Grounding: Phase 2 captures the structural conditions under which trafficked labor can be profitably deployed and sustained within a functioning economy. The central theoretical problem is concealment: forced labor is only viable at scale when it can be embedded in ordinary economic activity without triggering detection. Phase 2 determines whether traffickers can profitably retain and extract value from recruited populations.
phaseDefinitions.Key References: Savona et al. (2014); ILO Global Estimates of Modern Slavery (2022); GITOC Organized Crime Index; ITUC Global Rights Index

phaseDefinitions.Definition: Where proceeds are retained and reinvested. Conditions that enable value extraction from trafficking operations.
phaseDefinitions.Theoretical Grounding: Phase 3 captures the structural conditions under which trafficking enterprises can extract, convert, and retain financial value. The central theoretical problem is financial concealment: proceeds are only sustainably valuable if they can be integrated into legitimate financial systems without triggering detection or asset recovery.
phaseDefinitions.Key References: FATF (Financial Action Task Force); Basel AML Index; Tax Justice Network; GFI (Global Financial Integrity)

phaseDefinitions.Definition: Where power is consolidated to perpetuate the loop. Dissolved as independent phase — modeled as directed interaction term.
phaseDefinitions.Theoretical Grounding: The trafficking lifecycle is a self-reinforcing loop. Phase 2 and Phase 3 scores jointly amplify Phase 1. This directed interaction replaces the prior Phase 4 composite. A country where all three phases score high has a structurally self-perpetuating trafficking system.
phaseDefinitions.Key References: Aronowitz et al. (2010); Savona et al. (2014); Phase 4 Dissolution Rationale document

mechanismClasses.Definition: Geographic and environmental conditions that create or concentrate risk through physical proximity, terrain, infrastructure, or ecological process.
mechanismClasses.Spatial Character: Inherently spatial; can be mapped at high resolution; varies continuously across space.
mechanismClasses.Domains Using This Class: 1.1 Structural Disruption, 1.2 Displacement & Transit, 2.2 Concealment Geography

mechanismClasses.Definition: Socioeconomic and demographic conditions that reduce individual or household capacity to resist, refuse, or exit exploitative arrangements.
mechanismClasses.Spatial Character: Partially spatial; typically available at national or admin-1 level; varies discretely by administrative unit.
mechanismClasses.Domains Using This Class: 1.3 Reduced Bargaining Power, 2.3 Sector Exposure, 2.5 Structural Entrapment, 3.2 Laundering Capacity, 3.3 Cash & Informal Reliance, 3.4 Financial Infrastructure Access

mechanismClasses.Definition: Institutional, regulatory, and enforcement conditions that determine whether protective mechanisms are functional or absent.
mechanismClasses.Spatial Character: Weakly spatial at construction; typically national-level data; spatial variation emerges only at Layer 3 through subnational disaggregation where available.
mechanismClasses.Domains Using This Class: 1.5 Institutional Protection Deficit, 2.1 Enforcement Capacity, 2.4 Institutional Coercion Enablement, 3.1 Financial Architecture Opacity

scoreInterpretation.Interpretation: Higher values = higher structural risk on that specific dimension. 0 = lowest observed risk; 1 = highest observed risk. Most indicators are min-max normalized within year.
scoreInterpretation.Important Caveats: Scores are RELATIVE within the sample of countries. A score of 0.5 does not mean '50% risk' — it means halfway between the lowest and highest observed values.

scoreInterpretation.Interpretation: Equal-weighted average of constituent indicator scores. Higher = higher risk on that structural domain. Prefixed with R: (Recruitment), E: (Exploitation), or M: (Monetization).
scoreInterpretation.Important Caveats: Missing indicators excluded via pairwise available method. Domains with fewer available indicators may be less reliable for a given country.

scoreInterpretation.Interpretation: Equal-weighted average of domain scores within that phase. Captures the overall structural risk environment for that stage of the trafficking lifecycle.
scoreInterpretation.Important Caveats: Phase scores summarize multiple domains. Two countries with the same phase score may have very different domain-level profiles.

scoreInterpretation.Interpretation: Directed interaction term: how much Phase 2 × Phase 3 amplify or dampen Phase 1 recruitment risk. Positive = amplification (self-reinforcing loop); Negative = dampening.
scoreInterpretation.Important Caveats: Not a standalone risk measure. Represents the FEEDBACK mechanism — how exploitation and monetization conditions regenerate recruitment vulnerability.

scoreInterpretation.Interpretation: Recruitment score after applying the reinforcement effect. Countries with high exploitation AND monetization see amplified recruitment risk.
scoreInterpretation.Important Caveats: This is the score used in the final composite, not raw RECRUITMENT.

scoreInterpretation.Interpretation: Final composite risk score combining all three phases with reinforcement adjustment. Higher = higher structural risk of forced labor at the country level.
scoreInterpretation.Important Caveats: CRITICAL: This measures STRUCTURAL RISK ENVIRONMENT, not prevalence of forced labor. A high score means conditions are structurally favorable for trafficking — not that trafficking is necessarily occurring at that level.

scoreInterpretation.Interpretation: Rank order where 1 = highest structural risk. Based on COMPOSITE INDEX score.
scoreInterpretation.Important Caveats: Rankings should be interpreted with attention to the score differences between adjacent countries. Small score differences may produce large rank differences in the middle of the distribution.

aggregationMethodology.Name: Domain Score
aggregationMethodology.Formula / Method: Equal-weighted arithmetic mean of constituent indicators within domain
aggregationMethodology.Description: Each domain score is the simple average of its normalized (0-1) indicator scores. Missing indicators are excluded (pairwise available method).
aggregationMethodology.Notes: Formative measurement model: each indicator captures something conceptually unique. VIF < 5 diagnostic.

aggregationMethodology.Name: Phase Score
aggregationMethodology.Formula / Method: Equal-weighted arithmetic mean of domain scores within phase
aggregationMethodology.Description: Phase scores (RECRUITMENT, EXPLOITATION, MONETIZATION) are the simple average of their constituent domain scores.
aggregationMethodology.Notes: Phase 1 has 4 domains in current aggregation; Phase 2 has 5 domains; Phase 3 has 4 domains.

aggregationMethodology.Name: Reinforcement Effect
aggregationMethodology.Formula / Method: Directed interaction: P2 × P3 jointly modify P1
aggregationMethodology.Description: RECRUITMENT (adjusted) = RECRUITMENT × f(EXPLOITATION, MONETIZATION). The reinforcement effect captures the self-reinforcing trafficking loop where exploitation and monetization conditions regenerate recruitment vulnerability.
aggregationMethodology.Notes: Phase 4 dissolved as independent phase. Feedback modeled as directed interaction term: P2 + P3 → P1.

aggregationMethodology.Name: RECRUITMENT (adjusted)
aggregationMethodology.Formula / Method: P1_adj = P1 × (1 + k × (P2 × P3 - mean(P2 × P3)))
aggregationMethodology.Description: Recruitment score adjusted by the reinforcement effect. Countries where exploitation AND monetization are both high see amplified recruitment risk. Countries where either is low see dampened recruitment risk.
aggregationMethodology.Notes: Geometric mean interaction form selected at Stage 3. Directionality: P2 and P3 jointly amplify P1, not symmetric.

aggregationMethodology.Name: COMPOSITE INDEX
aggregationMethodology.Formula / Method: Weighted combination of adjusted Recruitment + Exploitation + Monetization
aggregationMethodology.Description: Final composite index score combining the three phase scores with the reinforcement adjustment applied to recruitment. Produces a single 0-1 risk score per country.
aggregationMethodology.Notes: Higher = higher structural risk of forced labor. Score does NOT measure prevalence — it measures the structural environment enabling trafficking.

aggregationMethodology.Name: COMPOSITE RANK
aggregationMethodology.Formula / Method: Rank order of COMPOSITE INDEX (1 = highest risk)
aggregationMethodology.Description: Countries ranked by composite index score. Rank 1 = highest structural forced labor risk.
